17 - The Secret to Turning Anyone Into a Buyer - YouTube.mp4
Stop qualifying the prospect to see if they're qualified for what you sell. You want to do this one thing instead. This is Jedi mind stuff. You want to do this. You want to build the prospect into this, the identity of the person who becomes a buyer. Now, why is that critical for you to match? Because most salespeople try to ask qualifying questions to see if a prospect is qualified for what they offer, because maybe they're not looking for that. Or maybe they don't have the funds or funding, and they try to qualify too early. Here's the reality. Unless you're selling a $10 million solution to a homeless person. Most people can find the funds or funding if they really want to, if you learn how to build them into the identity of the person who wants that.
And I'm going to show you a few. Frames you can use to actually start doing that. OK, because it's not like the money does not exist. They're already spending that current money on something else that, in their mind, is more of a priority than what you were offering. So how do I shift that way of thinking, that identity, from where they're spending that current money and start getting them to spend it on what you're offering, your solutions, so you can solve their problems. And I want you to write this down. Sigmund Freud said it best. People do who they believe they are. Let me repeat that. People do who they believe they are. So what he was saying in a shorter version is that people make decisions based on the identity of who they believe they are.
So if their identity believes, 'I never make a rash decision' and you're selling to a consumer in a one-call close, or if they're a business. My identity is, I believe we have to get six different quotes. That is their belief system, their identity. So what objections are you always going to run up against? Those exact ones. I don't make rash decisions or we need to get more quotes. Okay. So I need to take them out of that identity and rebuild them into an identity who makes decisions quickly. You want me to show you a technique how to do that, but you're not doing it. To them you're doing it for them because if they don't buy, you can't solve their problems, they don't get where they want. Okay.
So let me give you a few examples. And this is called an NEPQ identity frame. Now, if you're not one of our clients, NEPQ stands for neuro emotional persuasion questioning. Okay. Methodology I developed. Studying behavioral science and neuropsychology at university. Okay. So there are some tweaks to this based on what you sell. So let's say that you're into the conversation. This is not something you're going to say. Like the first question or the first words out of your mouth. You know, you're going to ask some connection questions, two, three connection questions, and then I can get into this maybe a few minutes. Into the conversation as I'm opening them up. Because what's your goal as a salesperson? You want the prospect to open up to you, right?
Because if they're closed off, if they're surface-level. Are you really going to make the sale? Unlikely, because they don't open up emotionally. So how do I get them open up emotionally? Here's a technique to do that. So let's say, asked a few questions, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah. You know, what was it? about the ad that attracted your attention, whatever you do. from fear. I mean, we all know that, you know, people like that who can't open up about their problems are never going to be able to really scale their business. You know what I mean? Yeah, yeah. I can say, thought they knew everything there was to know about how to run a business, whatever it is. See, this is what's called a negative. identity. Does anybody want to be this person? You know, the type like so closed off, one word answers, thought they knew everything there was to know about blank. And we all know that. That just comes from fear.
We all know that companies like that— who can't open up about their problems— are never going to be able to scale. Or we all know that people like that— who can't open up about their issues— are never going to get them solved. Whatever you want to say, that is a negative identity. No one you're talking to wants to be associated with the person who's closed off— out of fear— who never has success or who never gets what they want. There are some tweaks based off what you sell. Okay. Nobody wants to be in this identity now. If I'm a salesperson, notice how I'm structuring this. Do I want my prospects closed off? Yes or no? No. I want them open. See how I'm doing that. them to give me one word answers? No, you want them to open up and answer stuff. You want them to go below the surface. Does anybody want to be associated with a person who has fear? I want my prospects to tell me their problems. Yes. Do you see how I'm getting them to not want to be associated with this type of identity?
